_doc: Study notes derived from Jeremy's IT Lab Day 12 — Routers + Forwarding (CCNA objective 1.1). Bold key terms, bullets, key takeaways. NOT raw transcript.
transcriptTitle: The Life of a Packet — Routers and Forwarding

sections.title: CCNA 1.1 Cheat Sheet - Network Devices
bullets: This is the fast-pass objective: identify the **role and function** of common network components.
**Client** = requests a service.
**Server** = provides a service.
**Endpoint** = source or destination device such as a PC, laptop, phone, printer, IP phone, camera, or server.
**Switch** = connects hosts inside the same LAN.
**Router** = connects different networks.
**Firewall** = permits or denies traffic based on configured policy.
**NGFW** = next-generation firewall with advanced inspection/filtering.
**IPS** = detects and blocks malicious traffic.
**AP** = connects wireless clients to the wired LAN.
**Controller/WLC** = centrally manages network devices, especially APs.
**PoE** = provides electrical power over the Ethernet cable.
sections.keyTakeaway: Do not memorize boxes by appearance. Memorize the job: client asks, server provides, switch stays local, router goes remote, firewall enforces policy.

sections.title: Exam Trigger Words
bullets: "Connect many PCs" -> **switch**.
"Same LAN" or "many Ethernet ports" -> **switch**.
"Connect separate networks" or "Internet/WAN" -> **router**.
"Remote destination" or "default gateway" -> **router**.
"Allow/deny/filter/inspect" -> **firewall**.
"Advanced firewall filtering" -> **NGFW**.
"Prevent attacks" -> **IPS**.
"Wireless clients" -> **AP**.
"Manage many APs" -> **controller/WLC**.
"Power through Ethernet" -> **PoE**.
"Requests data" -> **client**.
"Provides data" -> **server**.
sections.keyTakeaway: The wording usually gives away the component. Read for the job the device is performing.

sections.title: Clients, Servers, And Endpoints
bullets: Client and server are **roles**, not permanent hardware categories.
The requester is the **client**.
The provider is the **server**.
A phone can be a client while watching YouTube and a server while sending an AirDrop file.
An **endpoint** or **end host** is a final source/destination device: PC, laptop, phone, printer, IP phone, camera, or server.
sections.keyTakeaway: Ask one question: who requested it, and who provided it?

sections.title: Switch vs Router vs Firewall
bullets: **Switch = same LAN.** Use it to connect many local hosts.
**Router = different networks.** Use it when traffic must reach another LAN, WAN, or the Internet.
**Firewall = policy.** Use it when traffic must be allowed, denied, filtered, inspected, or protected.
Router question = where should traffic go?
Firewall question = should this traffic be allowed?
A Layer 2 switch forwards frames inside a LAN/VLAN. A Layer 3 switch can also route between VLANs or networks.
sections.keyTakeaway: Same LAN is switch. Remote network is router. Allow/block is firewall.

sections.title: APs, Controllers, And PoE
bullets: **Access point (AP)** = connects wireless clients to the wired LAN.
**Wireless LAN controller (WLC)** = centrally manages multiple APs.
**Network controller** = central management for network devices or policy.
**PoE** = Power over Ethernet; data and electrical power use the same Ethernet cable.
Common PoE-powered devices: APs, IP phones, security cameras.
sections.keyTakeaway: AP gives wireless access. Controller manages. PoE powers devices.

sections.title: Router = Layer-3 Device
bullets: Routers operate at **Layer 3** of the OSI model. They forward **packets** based on the **destination IP address** inside the packet header.
Each router interface terminates **its own subnet** and **its own broadcast domain**. Packets crossing subnets must be routed.
Router does three jobs on every packet: **decapsulate** the incoming frame, **look up** the destination IP in the routing table, **rewrite** the frame for the next hop and forward.
**Switches** are Layer 2 (forward by MAC inside one broadcast domain). **Hubs** are Layer 1 repeaters. Routers are the only devices that move packets *between* subnets.
sections.keyTakeaway: If destination IP is on a different subnet, you need a router. Routers break broadcast domains; switches do not.

sections.title: Reading `show ip route`
bullets: Output format: `<code>  <prefix> [<AD>/<metric>] via <next-hop>, <uptime>, <interface>`
Source codes (memorize these):
• **C** = directly **C**onnected subnet (AD 0)
• **L** = **L**ocal /32 host route for the router's own interface IP
• **S** = **S**tatic route (manually configured) — AD 1
• **S\*** = **S**tatic **default** route (gateway of last resort)
• **O** = learned via **O**SPF — AD 110
• **D** = learned via EIGRP (D for **D**iffusing Update Algorithm) — AD 90
• **R** = learned via **R**IP — AD 120
• **B** = learned via **B**GP — AD 20 (eBGP) or 200 (iBGP)
• **i** = IS-IS
Bracket field `[AD/metric]` — first number is **administrative distance**, second is the protocol's **metric** (cost).
sections.keyTakeaway: Reading the routing table fluently = exam superpower. The single letter on the left tells you how the route got there and the trust level.

sections.title: Longest Prefix Match — The #1 Rule
bullets: When a packet arrives, the router compares the destination IP against **every** route in the table simultaneously.
**Most specific (longest) prefix wins** — always. A /30 beats a /24, a /24 beats a /16.
Example: destination `192.168.5.7`, routing table has `192.168.0.0/16` and `192.168.5.0/24`. Router picks **192.168.5.0/24** because /24 > /16.
Administrative distance and metric are **NOT** consulted unless two routes have **identical prefix length** AND come from different sources.
The longest-prefix-match algorithm is implemented in hardware (TCAM on real Cisco gear) — it's effectively O(1).
sections.keyTakeaway: More specific = always wins. AD and metric are tiebreakers, not primary deciders.

sections.title: Administrative Distance (AD)
bullets: AD is the **trust score** for a route's source. Lower AD = more trusted = preferred.
Standard values (memorize):
• **0** — Directly Connected (C)
• **1** — Static route (S)
• **90** — EIGRP (internal)
• **255** — Unknown / unreachable (route is **unusable**)
When the **same prefix** is learned from **two protocols** at the same time (e.g., OSPF + RIP both advertise 10.1.1.0/24), the router installs the route with the **lower AD** into the routing table.
The losing route is **not deleted** — it stays in the protocol's local database and gets promoted if the winner disappears.
sections.keyTakeaway: AD only matters when the prefix lengths are equal. Otherwise longest-prefix-match has already decided.

sections.title: Metric — Within-Protocol Tiebreaker
bullets: Metric breaks ties when **multiple routes from the SAME source** exist for the same prefix (e.g., two OSPF paths to 10.1.1.0/24).
Each protocol calculates metric differently:
• **OSPF** — total path cost (sum of interface costs along the path; reference bandwidth / interface bandwidth)
• **EIGRP** — composite of bandwidth, delay, load, reliability, MTU (default uses bandwidth + delay only)
• **RIP** — hop count (max 15 hops; 16 = unreachable)
• **Static** — administrator can set metric manually (default 0; higher value = floating static route used as backup)
sections.keyTakeaway: AD picks between protocols. Metric picks within a protocol. Both only after longest-prefix-match.

sections.title: Default Route — `0.0.0.0/0`
bullets: The **default route** matches every destination IP not covered by a more specific entry. Prefix is `0.0.0.0/0` — zero bits long, so longest-prefix-match always rejects it first if anything else matches.
Configured statically: `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 <next-hop>` — appears in `show ip route` as **S\***.
Can also be learned dynamically via OSPF (`default-information originate`), EIGRP, or BGP — appears with that protocol's code prefixed with `*`.
**Gateway of last resort** is the device the default route points to. `show ip route` shows this on its own line near the top.
If no default route exists AND no specific match is found, the router **drops the packet** and may send back an **ICMP Destination Unreachable**.
sections.keyTakeaway: S\* is your most-used static. Without it, the router silently drops packets to unknown destinations.

sections.title: Connected (C) and Local (L) Routes
bullets: **C** routes appear automatically when an interface is **up/up** and has an IP address. The route covers the **subnet** the interface is on.
**L** routes are **/32 host routes** for the **router's own interface IP**. They tell the CPU 'this packet is for me — punt to the control plane'.
Example: configure GigabitEthernet0/1 with `10.0.0.1/30`. Two routes appear:
• `C   10.0.0.0/30 is directly connected, GigabitEthernet0/1`
• `L   10.0.0.1/32 is directly connected, GigabitEthernet0/1`
When you `shutdown` the interface, **both** entries disappear from the routing table.
Connected routes have **AD 0** — they always win.
sections.keyTakeaway: C = subnet, L = router's own IP /32. The L routes are why the router knows packets are addressed to itself.

sections.title: ARP + Frame Rewrite at Each Hop
bullets: When a router forwards a packet out an interface, it must build a **new Ethernet frame** with the correct **destination MAC**.
Router uses **ARP** to resolve the **next-hop IP** (from the routing table) to a MAC address. The result is cached in the **ARP table** (`show ip arp`).
The packet's **source MAC** becomes the router's **exit interface MAC**. The **destination MAC** becomes the next-hop's MAC (or the destination host's MAC if it's the final hop on the local subnet).
**Layer 2 changes at every hop** (MACs rewritten). **Layer 3 does NOT change** (source IP and destination IP stay the same end-to-end, except in NAT scenarios).
TTL (Time-To-Live) is decremented by 1 at each hop. TTL=0 → router drops + sends **ICMP Time Exceeded** back to source. Traceroute exploits this.
sections.keyTakeaway: L2 mutates per hop, L3 stays the same. ARP is how routers learn next-hop MACs. TTL prevents routing loops.

sections.title: The Full Forwarding Decision (Recap)
bullets: **1. Packet arrives** on an interface. Frame is decapsulated, IP header is read.
**2. Destination IP** is compared against the routing table using **longest-prefix-match**.
**3.** If no match AND no default route → **drop + ICMP Unreachable**.
**4.** If multiple routes have equal prefix length → use **lowest AD** to pick the source.
**5.** If still tied (same source, same prefix) → use **lowest metric**.
**6.** Look up the **next-hop IP** in the ARP table. If absent, send **ARP request**.
**7. Decrement TTL**. If TTL=0 → drop + ICMP Time Exceeded.
**8.** Build new Ethernet frame: source MAC = exit interface, destination MAC = next-hop MAC.
**9. Forward** the frame out the chosen interface.
sections.keyTakeaway: Memorize this 9-step sequence. Half of CCNA routing questions are 'what does the router do at step N?'
